rules: Enforce domain invariants via private setters and validation in constructor or fluent builder pattern
Encapsulate state: use private fields with public properties for controlled access
Never expose ICollection<T> directly; use IReadOnlyCollection<T> or return defensive copies
Implement business logic as domain methods (e.g., CanApplyDiscount(), MarkAsCompleted()), not just data container
Use value objects for complex properties (e.g., Address, Money, Email) instead of primitive strings
Document business rules and constraints as code comments or exception messages
Keep entity lightweight: domain logic, not persistence logic (no EF annotations in domain)
Use simple types: string, int, bool, DateTime, Guid (no entity references or nested DTOs)
Include ONLY fields needed for the API contract (responses) or command input
Use nullable types (?) for optional fields; document why each is optional
Make DTO immutable: all properties are { get; set; } OR use init-only setters (records preferred)
Apply data annotations for Swagger/validation: [Required], [MaxLength], [Range] for API contracts
Do NOT include IDs of related entities unless explicitly needed for filtering/linking
Map DTO ↔ Entity in Handlers or service layer, never in domain entity itself
Command class is a simple DTO: immutable, no logic
Include only fields required for the business operation (not UI convenience fields)
Use init-only setters (records preferred) to enforce immutability
Add XML documentation (///) on the class describing the business intent, not the data
Implement IRequest<T> where T is the DTO or Result<DTO> returned by the handler
No validation logic here; validation belongs in CommandValidator class
Command includes ID of entity to update plus fields being changed
Use init-only setters or records for immutability
Include only necessary fields; don't replicate the entire entity
Example: UpdateOrderCommand with OrderId, Status, EstimatedDelivery (not all 20 fields)
Implement IRequest<Result<DTO>>
Add clear XML documentation explaining the business operation
Delete command is minimal: typically just the ID (and version for optimistic concurrency if needed)
Example: DeleteOrderCommand(Guid OrderId, int? Version)
No need for other entity data; repository loads it for validation
Implement IRequest<Result<Unit>> (void result) unless returning deleted entity DTO
Query is a filter/search request: ID, search term, pagination, sorting
Example GetOrderListQuery: PageNumber, PageSize, Status, CustomerId, SortBy
Immutable: init-only setters or records
Implement IRequest<Result<List<DTO>>> or IRequest<Result<PagedResult<DTO>>>
No business logic; QueryHandler extracts, filters, and returns data
Inherit AbstractValidator<Create{{entity}}Command> from FluentValidation
Define rules in constructor using RuleFor(...).Must/NotEmpty/MaximumLength chains
Validate format: NotEmpty(), EmailAddress(), Custom() for complex rules
Validate business invariants: e.g., EndDate > StartDate, Quantity > 0, Status transitions
Use .WithMessage() with context-specific, user-friendly error messages
Keep validators focused: one validator per command/query type
Use private methods for reusable validation logic (e.g., IsValidPhoneNumber), defined inside class-level marker
Validate update constraints: some fields may not be editable in certain states
Example: Cannot change OrderStatus from Shipped back to Pending
Validate required fields and field formats (email, phone, length)
Use Custom() rule for async validation if needed (e.g., checking uniqueness)
Provide clear error messages: 'Status change not allowed: order is already shipped'
Validate delete preconditions: is entity deletable in current state?
Example: Cannot delete Order if shipment already initiated
Validate version/concurrency token if using optimistic locking
Keep validation lightweight; detailed checks belong in handler
Entry method: public async Task<Result<{{entity}}Dto>> Handle(Create{{entity}}Command request, CancellationToken ct)
Decompose into private methods: ValidateRequest(), LoadDependencies(), CreateEntity(), PersistChanges(), MapToDto()
Validate input (throw BusinessException for violations) → Load related entities → Create new domain entity → Persist via UnitOfWork → Map to DTO → Return
Use dependency injection (constructor): IRepository<T>, IUnitOfWork, IMapper, ILogger
Throw NotFoundException if required related entities not found
Catch DbUpdateConcurrencyException for optimistic locking; catch DbUpdateException for constraint violations
Return Result<{{entity}}Dto> (success or failure DTO, not exceptions); let middleware map to HTTP status
Load existing entity by ID first; throw NotFoundException if not found
Validate state transitions (e.g., can status change from current to requested?)
Apply updates to the EXISTING entity instance via setter or Update() method, not create new
Decompose logic: LoadEntity(), ValidateStateTransition(), ApplyChanges(), PersistChanges()
Use optimistic locking (version) if concurrent edits are possible; catch concurrency exceptions gracefully
Return Result<DTO> with updated entity mapped to DTO
Load entity by ID; throw NotFoundException if not found
Validate delete preconditions (state, related entities, business rules)
Delete via repository.Remove() (or custom soft-delete logic if needed)
Persist via UnitOfWork.SaveChangesAsync()
Return Result<Unit> (void result); log deletion for audit if required
Load entity by ID with eager-loaded related entities via .Include(e => e.Related)
Return NotFoundException if entity not found; do NOT return null DTO
Map entity to DTO using projection (Select(e => new Dto { ... })) or AutoMapper
Use IQueryable.FirstOrDefaultAsync() for efficiency (DB-side filtering, not in-memory)
Example: _context.Orders.Include(o => o.Items).FirstOrDefaultAsync(o => o.Id == request.Id)
Build query: var query = _context.{{plural entity}}.AsNoTracking().Where(...)
Filter by request parameters (SearchTerm, Status, CustomerId, DateRange)
Eager-load related entities: .Include(e => e.Related) to prevent N+1 queries
Apply sorting: .OrderBy(e => e.Name).ThenBy(e => e.Id) for deterministic ordering
Apply pagination LAST: .Skip(pageSize * (pageNumber - 1)).Take(pageSize)
Map to DTOs via projection (Select()) or AutoMapper
Return PagedResult<DTO>(items, totalCount, pageNumber, pageSize)
Repository is a persistence abstraction: single responsibility is data access
Inject DbContext<T> or DbContextFactory<T>; keep DbSet<T> private
Implement IRepository<T> interface methods: AddAsync, UpdateAsync, RemoveAsync, GetByIdAsync, GetAllAsync
For mutations (Add/Update/Remove): validate input (null/bounds), use DbContext methods, NOT custom SQL
For queries: build IQueryable<T>, use .AsNoTracking() for read-only scenarios, .Include() for eager loading
Return IQueryable<T> for queries (lazy evaluation); return Task<T> for commands (eager)
Use private helper methods (GetQueryable, ApplyFilters, ApplySort) for reusable query patterns
ALWAYS prevent N+1: document query complexity, use .Include() explicitly, consider projection for large result sets
Example: public async Task<Order?> GetByIdAsync(Guid id) => await _context.Orders.Include(o => o.Items).FirstOrDefaultAsync(o => o.Id == id)
Repository interface defines contracts for data operations, not implementation
Methods: AddAsync(T), UpdateAsync(T), RemoveAsync(T), GetByIdAsync(Guid), GetAllAsync()
Include specialized query methods: GetByCustomerIdAsync, SearchByNameAsync, etc.
Return types: Task<T?> for single entity, Task<IEnumerable<T>> or Task<List<T>> for collections
Do NOT include DbContext or DbSet in interface; repository hides persistence details
XML documentation must explain business intent, not implementation (e.g., 'Get orders for customer excluding cancelled')
Implement IEntityTypeConfiguration<T> from EF Core
Configure in Configure(EntityTypeBuilder<T> builder) method
MUST configure: .HasKey(e => e.Id) for primary key
Configure required fields: .Property(e => e.Name).IsRequired().HasMaxLength(200)
Configure indices for query performance: .HasIndex(e => e.Email).IsUnique()
Configure relationships: .HasMany(e => e.Items).WithOne(i => i.Order).HasForeignKey(i => i.OrderId)
Set cascade delete: .OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade) for sub-entities, Restrict for references
Use Value Conversions for enums: .Property(e => e.Status).HasConversion(v => v.ToString(), v => Enum.Parse<Status>(v))
NEVER put data seeding here; use separate Seeder class or migrations
UnitOfWork coordinates multiple repositories and manages transactions
Implement IUnitOfWork with property for each repository (IOrderRepository, ICustomerRepository, etc.)
Implement IAsyncDisposable; DbContext cleanup happens in Dispose()
Provide SaveChangesAsync() method that persists all changes from all repositories in one transaction
Transaction handling: SaveChangesAsync() wraps changes in DbContext.Database.BeginTransactionAsync() if needed
Example: public async Task SaveChangesAsync() => await _context.SaveChangesAsync();
Repositories share same DbContext instance; never create new contexts in repository methods
Controller is a thin HTTP adapter: route mapping, request parsing, response formatting
Inject IMediator (MediatR) and ILogger<T>
Route pattern: [ApiController] [Route("api/[controller]")] for RESTful conventions
Methods: [HttpGet("{id}")] GetById, [HttpGet] GetList, [HttpPost] Create, [HttpPut("{id}")] Update, [HttpDelete("{id}")] Delete
Return IActionResult or ActionResult<T>: Ok(dto), CreatedAtAction, NotFound, BadRequest, UnprocessableEntity(errors)
Use async Task methods: public async Task<ActionResult<OrderDto>> GetById(Guid id)
Send command/query via mediator: var result = await _mediator.Send(new GetOrderByIdQuery(id))
Map HTTP status codes: 200 Ok (GET, PUT), 201 CreatedAtAction (POST), 204 NoContent (DELETE), 404 NotFound, 400 BadRequest, 422 UnprocessableEntity (validation)
Return Result<T> from handler; convert to appropriate HTTP status in controller catch/switch block
MediatR behavior that runs validation for every command/query before handler
Check if IRequest has FluentValidation validators; run them if present
If validation fails, throw ValidationException with all errors (not just first)
Return error response with field-level details: { field: 'OrderTotal', message: 'must be > 0' }
Log validation failures for monitoring
MediatR behavior that logs command/query execution for auditing and debugging
Log at Info level: command/query name, execution time, result status
Log at Error level: exceptions with full context (what request caused it)
Include correlation ID or request ID for tracing across services
Example: Log.Information("Command {CommandName} completed in {ElapsedMs}ms", typeof(TRequest).Name, stopwatch.ElapsedMilliseconds)
Custom exception for domain/business entity not found (not HTTP 404, but semantic)
Inherit from ApplicationException or custom base
Include entity type and ID for debugging: NotFoundException(typeof(Order), orderId)
HTTP middleware catches and converts to 404 Not Found response
Message: 'Order with id {orderId} not found' (user-friendly, not stacktrace)
Custom exception for domain validation failures (business rule violations)
Include field-level errors: Dictionary<string, string[]> Errors { get; }
Example: new ValidationException("EndDate must be after StartDate", new { endDate = errors })
HTTP middleware catches and converts to 422 Unprocessable Entity response
Used by FluentValidation and custom validation logic

targets._comment: Brownfield project-reference wiring: an existing API project picks up the module's Application/Infrastructure references without csproj edits; the generated API project opts out via ScaffoldModuleProject
API project file, thin controller layer
Global usings for Application unit test project
Dev-only EnsureCreated hosted service for the module's ApplicationDbContext (works in greenfield and brownfield hosts alike)
Infrastructure layer DI setup, injection target REPOSITORIES
MVC exception filter (registered by AddApi) — maps module exceptions to 400/404 independent of the host's middleware pipeline
Program.cs entry point, injection target DI
Launch settings for IIS Express / Kestrel
Per-entity: Get by ID query handler
Per-entity: Get by ID handler unit test
Per-entity: Get list handler unit test

bootstrapAnchors._comment: DI registrations belong in the web host's builder zone
DbSet accumulation inside this pack's own DbContext class (AppDbContext accepted for older repos)
Repository registrations inside AddInfrastructure (this pack names the file DependencyInjection.cs; the legacy extensions filename is accepted too)
